actions.thought: Holding T7s on the button in a 3-handed game is a standard opening hand. I want to exert positional pressure and build a pot with a hand that has good post-flop playability. Raising to 3BB is a standard sizing to take down the blinds or see a flop with initiative.

76o is a very weak offsuit connector. Facing a 3x open from the Button, calling or 3-betting from the Small Blind with this hand is heavily unprofitable due to poor equity and being out of position post-flop. Folding is the standard and best play here.

Q4o is a very weak starting hand with poor playability, especially out of position. Even though the price is decent to call, defending such a dominated range against a button open is generally a losing strategy. It is best to fold and preserve my stack for better opportunities.

QJo is a strong starting hand in 3-handed play. As the first to act on the Button, I want to open-raise to steal the blinds or build a pot in position. A raise to 2.5x the big blind (250 chips) is a standard opening size that puts pressure on the blinds while keeping the pot manageable.

Holding Ace-Deuce suited in the small blind against a button open provides good equity to play post-flop. Calling is the standard play here as it keeps the pot size manageable while playing out of position, and I have the potential to hit a strong flush or two-pair combination.

I am in the Big Blind holding Jh6h. The Button (Pro) opened with a raise to 2.5x, and the Small Blind (Flashlight) called. I need to call 150 more into a total pot of 600, giving me 4:1 pot odds. Suited Jacks have decent playability post-flop, and given the excellent odds and the fact that I close the pre-flop action, a call is the most standard and profitable play.

I have flopped trip Aces with a deuce kicker. This is a very strong hand, but since I am acting first out of position against two opponents, a check is the most strategic play. It allows me to trap, gauge the strength of the original raiser (Pro) and the other caller (Flash), and keep the pot manageable while holding a hand that is likely the best but vulnerable to better-kicker Aces.

The flop is 5c-Ad-As, a very dry and paired board that heavily favors the pre-flop aggressor's range. I have Jack-high with no draws or pairs. In a multi-way pot, leading into the pre-flop raiser on this texture is ill-advised. The standard play is to check and see how the Button reacts, as they will likely continuation-bet frequently here. I have no reason to put more chips in the pot with this hand at this stage.

With QJ high on an A-A-5 board in a multi-way pot, I have very little equity if called. Although the board is dry and a continuation bet might fold out some better hands like King-high or small pocket pairs, betting into two opponents who called a pre-flop raise is risky as one of them could easily be slowplaying an Ace or a pocket pair. Checking back allows me to see a free turn card, keeps the pot small, and avoids spewing chips with air.

With trips on the turn and a gutshot straight draw, my hand has improved in relative strength and equity. Given the flop went check-check-check, it is unlikely my opponents have a strong Ace. Betting now is essential to extract value from smaller pairs or draws and to protect against potential free cards that could complete a straight or flush draw for my opponents.

I have Jack-high with no pair and no draw on a board of Ad-As-5c-4c. Flashlight has led out into two opponents after the flop was checked around. My hand has no showdown value and no realistic way to improve to the best hand. Continuing would be a significant mistake given the multi-way nature of the pot and my total lack of equity.

With Queen-Jack high and no draw on an A-A-5-4 board, I have essentially zero equity. Flashlight's lead on the turn indicates strength or a draw that I cannot beat. Calling is unprofitable, and bluff-raising is unnecessarily risky and spewy. Folding is the clear and correct play.

My starting hand, 2c-4s, is one of the weakest possible combinations in Texas Hold'em. It lacks high-card strength, connectivity, and suitedness, offering virtually no potential for equity on most boards. Given that I am in an early stage of the tournament with 150 hands to go, I should play disciplined poker and fold to avoid wasting chips on a non-competitive hand.

I am in the Small Blind with 9d 6h. The button has folded, leaving me heads-up against Pro in the Big Blind. While 96o is somewhat playable in a 3-handed context, it is generally too weak to complete or raise from the Small Blind against a competent opponent who will have position on me for the rest of the hand. Folding is the most disciplined play to avoid unnecessary losses with a marginal hand out of position.

I have pocket Jacks on the button in a 3-handed game, which is an extremely strong starting hand. My goal is to raise for value and to gain initiative in the hand. A standard 3x big blind raise to 300 is appropriate to build the pot and potentially take it down pre-flop or narrow the field.

We are in the Small Blind with King-Ten offsuit facing a 3x open from the Button. KTo plays poorly out of position and is easily dominated by the Button's calling or 4-betting ranges if we choose to 3-bet. Calling is also suboptimal due to our positional disadvantage and the Big Blind still acting behind us, who could potentially squeeze. The standard and most sound play here is to fold.

I am in the Big Blind with 8-3 offsuit, which is a very weak starting hand with poor playability post-flop. The pot odds do not justify calling a raise from the button with such a trash hand out of position. Folding is the most disciplined and mathematically sound decision here to preserve my stack for a better spot.

K2o is a weak offsuit hand, even in 3-handed play. While the button allows for wider opening ranges, K2o is easily dominated by better Kings and Aces, and plays poorly post-flop. With around 100 big blinds, there is no need to force a steal with such a marginal hand.

With Ace-Jack offsuit in the small blind and the button having folded, I have a clear advantage heads-up against the big blind. Raising allows me to capture the initiative and potentially win the pot immediately or set up a strong continuation bet on the flop.

I am in the Big Blind with J9o. Flashlight (Small Blind) raised to 300. Since I am in a 3-handed game, the Small Blind's opening range is typically very wide. I am being offered 2:1 odds on a call (200 to win a total pot of 400) and, crucially, I will have position on Flashlight for the remainder of the hand. J9o is well within the standard defending range in this spot. I will call to see a flop.

The board texture is dry and paired (3d-6c-6h), which is unlikely to have connected with the big blind's calling range. My Ace-Jack high is likely ahead or holds decent equity. A continuation bet of 250 into the 600 pot is a standard sizing to test the opponent's strength and potentially take down the pot immediately, as they likely missed this texture entirely.

On a paired board of 3d-6c-6h, the pre-flop aggressor (Flashlight) has made a continuation bet of 250 into a 600 pot. I hold Jack-high with two overcards (Jack and Nine). In a 3-handed game, the Small Blind's range is wide, and this dry board misses a large portion of that range. I am getting roughly 3.4:1 pot odds to call, and I have the advantage of position. Folding here would be overly tight, as I can still improve on the turn or take the pot away if he checks. I will call the bet to see a turn card.

The board is paired (3d-6c-6h) and the turn 8s does not improve my Ace-Jack high. My opponent called my continuation bet on the flop, which suggests they likely have a piece of the board (like a 6, an 8, or a pocket pair) or a decent draw. Betting here would effectively turn my hand into a bluff against a range that already showed interest on the flop. Checking is the prudent play to control the pot size and re-evaluate on the river.

Flashlight's check on the turn, following a pre-flop raise and a flop continuation bet, often indicates a marginal hand like Ace-high or a small pocket pair that is looking to control the pot, or a total miss that is giving up. While my Jack-high is unlikely to win at showdown right now, I have position and two overcards. I will check back to see a free river card, which allows me to either improve my hand or potentially bluff on the river if the board develops favorably and Flashlight checks again.

The board is 3d-6c-6h-8s-2h, which is a dry, non-flush board. My Ace-high is essentially a bluff-catcher that likely loses to any pair the opponent might have. Since we checked the turn, checking the river is the most logical play to get to showdown without risking more chips. Betting here would likely only be called by better hands, so checking minimizes potential losses while still allowing me to potentially win if the opponent missed completely.

Flashlight has checked on both the turn and the river after being the pre-flop aggressor and betting the flop. This sequence typically indicates a marginal hand that wants to get to showdown cheaply (like Ace-high or a small pocket pair) or a total give-up. My Jack-high has very little showdown value, but bluffing here after checking back the turn is difficult to sell, as most of my value range (like a 6 or an 8) would likely have bet the turn for protection or value. Given the dry board and the possibility that Flashlight might call a river bet with Ace-high or a small pair, I will check back and hope to catch a bluff or a lower high card, though I am likely losing this pot.

I hold 5h 2h, which is a very weak hand with low potential for hitting a strong pair or straight. In a three-handed game, even on the button, this hand lacks the equity to justify investing chips. Folding is the most disciplined and strategic move to preserve my stack.

I am in the Small Blind with 65o. The button has folded, leaving only me and the Big Blind. While 65o has some marginal connectivity, it is generally too weak to play out of position against the Big Blind. Raising to steal is an option, but given the early stage of the tournament and the lack of strong information on the Big Blind's defending range, folding is the most disciplined and standard play.

I am on the button with 9h 6h, a suited gapper that plays reasonably well post-flop, especially in position. With the blinds at 50/100, opening for a raise is a standard play to put pressure on the blinds and potentially take the pot down immediately. If called, I have the advantage of position for the rest of the hand. I'll raise to 300, which has been my standard opening size in previous hands.
I am in the Small Blind with 9c 5c. Flash raised to 3x from the button. This hand is too weak and disconnected to play from out of position against a raise. Folding is the optimal decision.

Holding 5-8 offsuit in the big blind against an aggressive button open is a clear fold. The hand has poor equity against a standard opening range, and playing post-flop out of position with such a weak hand would lead to difficult spots. It is better to conserve chips and wait for a more playable hand.
QJo is a solid starting hand on the button in 3-handed play. I'll open with a standard 2.5x raise to steal the blinds or play a pot in position if called.

I am in the Small Blind with Ace-Four offsuit. The Button has opened to 250, and I only need to call 200 more to see a flop. While playing out of position is a disadvantage, A4o is a reasonable hand to defend against a Button open, especially with effective stacks around 100 big blinds. I will call to see a flop and re-evaluate based on the textures.

I am in the Big Blind with J7s. The Button raised to 250 and the Small Blind called. I need to call 150 more into a pot that will be 750 total. This gives me 5-to-1 pot odds (or 20% equity needed). J7s is a solid speculative hand that plays well in multi-way pots, and I am closing the pre-flop action. Calling is the most standard and mathematically sound play here.

I am first to act in a 3-way pot on a coordinated board (2c-7d-8d) that favors the pre-flop aggressor's range. With only Ace-high and no significant draws, I have very little equity to continue if a bet is made. Checking is the standard play here to keep the pot small and re-evaluate my position against any future aggression.

I have middle pair (sevens) with a Jack kicker on a coordinated but relatively low board. In a three-way pot, leading out into the pre-flop aggressor (Pro) is generally not the best play with a marginal hand like this. Flashlight has already checked. By checking, I can see how Pro reacts. If Pro bets, I will have to evaluate the bet sizing, but middle pair is typically strong enough to call one street on this board. Checking keeps the pot manageable and allows me to see a turn card or catch bluffs from the button.

The 2c 7d 8d board heavily favors the calling ranges of the blinds, hitting pairs, straight draws, and flush draws. With just Queen-high and no direct draw, betting into two opponents here is risky with low fold equity. Checking behind is the best play to control the pot size and hopefully catch a J or Q on the turn, or re-evaluate.

I am first to act on the turn with Ace-high on a board of 2c-7d-8d-8s. Since the flop checked around, there is no indication that anyone has a strong hand, but my hand has very little showdown value and no equity to improve. Checking is the standard play here to keep the pot small, evaluate the opponents' reactions, and potentially get to a free showdown or fold to any significant aggression.

The 8s on the turn pairs the board, meaning I now have two pair (8s and 7s). Since the flop was checked around, it's unlikely anyone is sitting on a monster hand like an 8 or a high overpair. By checking again, I am maintaining a pot-control strategy with a hand that has solid showdown value but is vulnerable to a better 7 or a slow-played 8. I'll see if the pre-flop aggressor (Pro) decides to bet or check behind.

With both opponents checking again on the paired 8s turn, they are showing considerable weakness. However, I still only have Queen-high with no draw, and betting into two players remains risky as one of them could easily be trapping with an 8 or holding a sticky hand like a 7, a small pocket pair, or a stubborn Ace-high. Taking a free card gives me a chance to spike a Jack or Queen on the river, or I can reassess the situation to possibly bluff if the river checks through.

I am in the small blind on the river with Ace-high on a board of 2c-7d-8d-8s-Qc. The action has been checked around on both the flop and the turn, which suggests that no one holds a strong value hand like an eight or a pocket pair higher than 7. My Ace-high is essentially a bluff-catcher that has very low showdown value against a range that might contain a Queen or a pocket pair. Betting here would likely only be called by better hands and would require me to fold to a raise. Checking is the most prudent decision to see a showdown for free, as it is unlikely that any of my opponents will call a bet with a worse hand, and checking allows me to potentially win at showdown if everyone is also unimproved.

The board has been checked down since the flop. I have two pair, 8s and 7s, with a Jack kicker. Given the passive action, it's unlikely anyone has an 8. The Queen on the river is an overcard that could have hit the pre-flop aggressor (Pro) or the small blind, but there's no reason to bet into them and risk a raise when I have solid showdown value. By checking, I can see a cheap showdown and likely win against missed high cards or lower pairs.

Both opponents have shown weakness by checking down every street. The river Queen gives me top pair (Queens and Eights), which is almost certainly the best hand here. I should make a value bet to extract chips from smaller pairs like 7x, underpairs (99-JJ), or a stubborn Ace-high that might decide to bluff-catch since all the obvious draws (diamonds, straights) missed.

I am facing a bet of 350 into a pot of 750 on a board of 2c-7d-8d-8s-Qc. My hand is Ace-high. While I checked the previous streets, the Pro's delayed bet on the river often signifies that they have connected with the Queen or are value-betting a medium pair that they checked down earlier. Given the board texture and the fact that I only have Ace-high with no showdown value against any pair, calling is a losing play. I do not have enough equity to justify a call, and folding is the correct disciplined decision.

The board has been checked down until the river, where the pre-flop aggressor (Pro) finally bets 350 into a 750 pot. My hand, two pair (8s and 7s), is a strong bluff-catcher in this situation. The Queen on the river is a perfect card for Pro to represent after the previous passive streets. Given that I am getting better than 3-to-1 pot odds and the action suggests a wide range for Pro including missed diamond draws or high-card hands that didn't want to bet earlier, calling is the most profitable decision to see the showdown.

K-3 offsuit is a weak hand with poor playability, especially in a three-handed game. There is no benefit to entering the pot with such a weak holding from the button without any initiative. I will fold and wait for a more profitable spot.

I am in the Small Blind with 93o. The Button (Flashlight) has folded, leaving only me and the Big Blind (Pro). 93o is a very weak hand that plays poorly post-flop, especially when out of position. While it only costs 50 more to call, the potential to lose more chips post-flop outweighs the small investment. Given the opponent 'Pro' is likely to be competent, I will fold and wait for a better hand.

I am on the button with a pocket pair of fives (5c 5h) in a three-handed game. Pocket pairs have significant value, and being on the button gives me the advantage of position for the rest of the hand. I will open with a standard 3x raise to 300 to take control of the pot and potentially win the blinds or set-mine if called.

Facing a 3bb open from the button. While K5s has some playability and our opponent has shown a tendency to raise the button frequently, playing a weak suited King from the Small Blind out of position is a long-term losing proposition unless we use it as a 3-bet bluff. Without a read on how Flash handles 3-bets, folding is the most solid, low-variance play.

The opponent (Flash) has raised from the button. Holding Ace-Ten offsuit in the Big Blind, this is a standard defend. While I could consider a 3-bet to exert pressure, calling allows me to see a flop with a hand that has good equity against a wide button opening range while keeping the pot manageable and seeing a flop in position relative to the blinds.

I am in the Big Blind and flopped top pair (Ace) with a solid Ten kicker on a board of 8h-Js-Ac. Since the button raised pre-flop, they have a range advantage and are the likely aggressor. Donk-betting here would be a mistake as it narrows my range and makes it harder to extract value or control the pot. Checking is the standard play, allowing the opponent to continuation bet with their range, which I can then call with my top pair to evaluate future streets.

I was the pre-flop aggressor and the board came Ace-high, which is generally better for my perceived range than the Big Blind's defending range. Although my pocket fives are likely behind if Flashlight has any piece of the board, a continuation bet allows me to represent the Ace and take down the pot if Flashlight missed. I will bet around 40% of the pot.

I have top pair with an Ace and a solid Ten kicker on a board of 8h-Js-Ac. The button aggressor has continued their aggression with a 250 bet. This is a standard call; raising would likely force out hands I am beating (like lower Aces or pocket pairs) while isolating me against hands that have me beat, such as AJ, JJ, or 88. Calling keeps the pot size under control and allows me to re-evaluate on the turn.

On the turn, with the board showing 8h-Js-Ac-5d, I have top pair (Ace) with a solid Ten kicker. Checking is the most prudent play to maintain pot control and realize equity. The aggressor on the button has position and a wide range, so checking allows me to either check-call a reasonable bet to evaluate the river or take a free card if they check back. I do not have enough strength to lead out and bloat the pot, as that would only isolate me against better hands or fold out worse ones that I might want to keep in.

I have turned a set of fives, which is a very strong hand on this board. Flashlight's check-call on the flop indicates they likely have an Ace, a Jack, or perhaps a middle pair. Since Flashlight has checked again on the turn, I will place a value bet of 750, which is approximately two-thirds of the pot, to build the pot and extract maximum value from their range.

On the turn, with the board showing 8h-Js-Ac-5d, my top pair with a ten kicker remains a strong hand. While Flash's bet of 750 into a 1150 pot is aggressive, it does not necessarily indicate a superior hand (he could be betting a Jack, an Eight, or even a weaker Ace). Raising at this point would likely commit me to a large pot against hands that have me crushed, such as AJ or sets like 88 or JJ, while folding out hands that I currently beat. Calling is the most balanced approach, allowing me to keep the pot manageable and evaluate the river. If a clean card lands, I will be well-positioned to reassess.

I have reached the river with top pair (Aces with a Ten kicker) on a board of 8h-Js-Ac-5d-6c. Given the aggression from the button on the turn, I have decided that checking is the most optimal play to reach a showdown. Betting would be risky as it would likely only be called by better hands or hands that tie, while folding is not an option given the strength of top pair. Checking allows me to keep the pot size controlled and see the opponent's cards.

I have a set of fives on a board of 8h Js Ac 5d 6c. This is a very strong hand as there are no completed flushes and the only likely straights (like 7-9 or 4-7) are improbable given the pre-flop and post-flop action. Flashlight has check-called the flop and turn, which strongly suggests they have a piece of the board, likely a top pair with an Ace or a Jack. Since they checked the river, I will place a value bet to extract more chips from their range. A bet of 1500 into a 2650 pot is roughly 57% of the pot, which is a standard sizing to get called by an Ace.

I am facing a bet of 1500 into a pot that has grown to 2650 before the bet. With top pair and a Ten kicker (AdTs) on a board of 8h-Js-Ac-5d-6c, my hand is a classic bluff-catcher. Flash's line has been consistent, but it is certainly possible for them to be overvaluing a weaker Ace (like A9 or lower) or turning a missed straight draw into a bluff. Given the pot odds (calling 1500 to win a total pot of 4150), I only need about 36% equity to break even on a call. Folding would be too conservative given I have top pair, and raising is non-optimal as it effectively turns my hand into a bluff and would likely only get called by better hands. Therefore, calling is the most profitable decision to showdown.

I am on the Button with 9s 6c, which is a very weak and disconnected offsuit hand. Even with positional advantage, it does not hold enough equity or playability to justify a steal attempt or a call. The standard and most optimal play is to fold.

I am in the Small Blind with 5h 2h, which is a very weak hand. Since the Button has folded, I am only facing the Big Blind. While the price to call is cheap (50 chips into a 150-chip pot), 52s plays poorly out of position, and my opponent has shown a tendency to play aggressively. Folding is the most disciplined and conservative approach to protect my stack.

I am in the button position with Jack-Ten suited, which is a premium suited connector that performs well in position. I want to build the pot and potentially take control of the hand pre-flop or on the flop. A standard raise to 300 (3BB) is appropriate to open the action.

I am in the Small Blind with Jack-Six offsuit. The Button (Flashlight) has raised to 300. Jh 6s is a weak hand that does not play well out of position. Folding is the standard and most profitable play here to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

K9 offsuit is a marginal hand that plays poorly out of position. Facing a 3x raise from the Button, defending by calling or 3-betting is generally unprofitable, making folding the standard and most optimal play.

I am on the button in a 3-handed game with 9h 5h. Suited hands with some connectivity have significant value in short-handed play, especially from the button where I can exert positional pressure. I have had success with a 3x opening raise in previous hands, and both blinds have shown a tendency to fold to button aggression in earlier rounds. Opening for 300 is a standard play to take control of the pot and potentially win the blinds immediately or play a pot in position.
72 suited is one of the weakest hands in Texas Hold'em. Facing a raise from the Button, I am out of position and have terrible equity. Folding is the only correct play here to preserve my stack.

Queen-Eight offsuit is a marginal hand that plays poorly out of position. While I am getting reasonable pot odds to call a 3x raise, the disadvantage of playing OOP against an aggressive button-raiser like Flash outweighs the immediate pot equity. To maintain a disciplined stack management strategy, I will fold and wait for a stronger starting hand or a better positional advantage.

King-three offsuit is a very weak starting hand, well outside standard opening ranges even from the Button in a 3-handed game. With active opponents left to act in the blinds, attempting to steal the pot with a weak, easily dominated hand is not a profitable long-term strategy. The mathematically sound choice is to simply fold.

I am in the small blind with a pair of nines, which is a strong hand heads-up against the big blind. Since the button has folded, I am in a prime position to open the pot to put pressure on the big blind and hopefully win the pot immediately or play a post-flop hand with the betting lead. Raising to 300 is a standard sizing to accomplish this.

I have Ace-Deuce offsuit in the Big Blind. The Small Blind raised to 300. With an Ace and being in position for the rest of the hand, I have enough equity and tactical advantage to call the 200 more. I am getting 2:1 odds on a call, and my hand plays reasonably well against a wide Small Blind opening range.

I have a pair of nines on a board with two overcards, a Jack and a Ten. Being out of position as the pre-flop aggressor, checking is the most prudent play to control the pot size. Betting into the Big Blind on this board texture risks getting called or raised by a wider range of hands that connect well with the high cards, whereas checking allows me to re-evaluate on the turn while keeping my defensive range wide.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-high on a board of Tc-3h-Js. The pre-flop raiser (Flashlight) checked to me. While I have an overcard (the Ace), this board texture is likely to have hit a significant portion of a Small Blind opening range (pairs of Jacks or Tens). By checking behind, I take a free turn card, preserve my stack, and avoid bloating the pot with a weak hand that has limited semi-bluff equity. If the opponent checks again on the turn, I can re-evaluate my position.

The Ace on the turn is a bad card for my pair of nines, as it connects heavily with the opponent's range and makes my hand extremely vulnerable. There is no value in betting here, as I will likely only be called by hands that have me beaten. Checking is the most prudent strategy to control the pot size and re-evaluate on the river, effectively playing this as a check-fold scenario if the opponent shows strength.

On the turn, the Ace of Hearts hits, giving me top pair. The Small Blind (Flashlight) has checked twice now (on the flop and the turn). While my kicker is very weak (Deuce), my Ace is likely the best hand given the passive line taken by the opponent. I will bet 300 (half the pot) to extract value from a Jack, a Ten, or middle pairs that might call one street thinking I am bluffing after the flop went check-check. This also protects my hand against potential straight draws like KQ.

The turn card, an Ace, significantly weakens my pocket nines as it hits a large portion of my opponent's range. Given that the opponent bet after I checked, they are signaling strength, likely representing at least an Ace or a higher pocket pair. With no realistic equity to improve and being out of position, calling is a losing proposition. Folding is the most disciplined play to protect my stack for future hands.

3c 9c is a weak, uncoordinated hand with no significant equity. As the button in a 3-handed game, playing such marginal cards is generally suboptimal. Folding allows me to preserve my stack of 7400 and wait for a more profitable situation.

King-Jack suited is a premium starting hand, especially in a blind-vs-blind situation. With the button folding, I have a significant range advantage over the big blind. I will raise to 300 to build the pot and potentially win the blinds immediately.

Flash raises to 3x from the Small Blind, which he has done frequently, indicating a wide range. Pocket eights is a strong hand blind-versus-blind, well ahead of his opening range. While 3-betting for value and protection is a viable option, calling is also highly profitable as it keeps his weaker hands in the pot, guarantees we see a flop in position, and keeps the variance lower. I will call to evaluate the flop, looking to hit a set or navigate a low-card board.

I have an open-ended straight draw (K-Q-J-T) with two overcards. This is a very strong semi-bluffing candidate. Since I was the pre-flop aggressor from the Small Blind, this flop of Q-T-6 fits my perceived range well. I will lead out with a continuation bet of 350 to maintain initiative, build the pot in case I hit my draw, and potentially take the pot down immediately if the Big Blind missed.

The flop brings two overcards to our pocket eights (Queen and Ten). Flash is an aggressive player who frequently c-bets and barrels multiple streets. While he could be betting with air or straight draws like KJ or J9, our hand is highly vulnerable, effectively a marginal third pair with no straight or flush draws. Floating this flop bet in position is risky because most turn cards will be bad for us, and we will likely face continued aggression. Folding is the most disciplined and standard decision here.

In a 3-handed game, the Button should open a wide range of hands to put pressure on the blinds. However, Ten-Four suited (T4h) is at the very bottom of the opening range. While I have a significant chip lead and have been playing aggressively, T4h is likely to get me into trouble if called by either of the blinds, who have shown they are willing to defend. Given my current stack and the tournament stage, I prefer to wait for a hand with better post-flop playability or higher equity. Folding here preserves my lead and maintains a strong table image.

With QJo in the Small Blind and the Button folding, I have a strong enough hand to open-raise against the Big Blind. Raising to 3x the big blind puts pressure on the opponent and often wins the pot immediately or gives me initiative going to the flop.

I am in the big blind with Ace-Five suited, which is a strong enough holding to defend against a small blind raise. The pot odds offer 2:1 for the call (200 to win 400), and I will be in position post-flop. Calling is the most disciplined and standard play to control the pot size and realize equity with this hand.

I flopped top pair with a Jack kicker and a gutshot straight draw on a rainbow board. The board is quite connected and draw-heavy, so giving free cards is dangerous. A continuation bet of half the pot (300 chips) extracts value from worse pairs, straight draws, or floats, while effectively protecting my equity.

I am facing a bet on a coordinated Queen-Ten-Eight board with only Ace-high. This board texture hits the Small Blind's opening range very hard, and my hand provides no significant equity, showdown value, or draw potential. Calling a bet here would be burning chips without a clear path to winning the pot. Folding is the most disciplined and mathematically sound decision given the situation.
K6o is a weak offsuit hand. While we are on the button, it's generally too weak to open-raise profitably against two opponents, and we lack post-flop playability. Folding is the standard play here.
I am in the small blind with a weak hand (4c 6h). As I am out of position against the big blind and the hand has very little equity, the most disciplined play is to fold and preserve my stack.

Holding 4-7 offsuit is extremely weak and lacks playability. Being in the small blind out of position against a button raise makes this a clear fold, as there is no strategic value in continuing with such a marginal holding against two opponents.

3c 6d is an extremely weak starting hand with no tactical value. As the first to act from the button in a three-handed game, there is no strategic reason to enter the pot with this holding. Folding is the disciplined play to conserve my stack and wait for a stronger hand.

I have middle pair (nines) on a moderately dynamic board containing a flush draw and a Jack overcard. I am in the big blind with a pair of nines on a board of 5d 9d Js Jc. Being out of position against an aggressive button player like Flash, checking is the optimal strategy to control the pot size and see a free river card, rather than leading into strength or folding prematurely while I still have some showdown value.

On the turn, the board has paired Jacks (5d 9d Js Jc). I hold the nut flush draw with Ace-high. Flashlight has checked to me for the second time. I am holding a pair of nines on a board of 5d-9d-Js-Jc-Ah. On the river, the Ace of Hearts gives me top pair (Aces) with an eight kicker. The board is 5d-9d-Js-Jc-Ah. Flashlight has checked to me for the third time (including the turn). Given the Ace is a scare card, a check-back is a safe and effective way to claim the pot with my showdown value.

62 offsuit is one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em. Being first to act pre-flop, there is no justification to enter the pot with such a poor hand. The optimal play is to fold and wait for better opportunities to invest my stack.

I have flopped Two Pair (Sevens and Deuces) with a Queen kicker on a 2h-7h-2c board. My opponent in the Small Blind checked to me after raising pre-flop. 4c 2d is one of the weakest possible starting hands in Texas Hold'em. With no investment in the pot yet as the button, the most disciplined play is to fold rather than limp or raise with such poor equity, especially with two opponents left to act.

I have bottom pair (sevens) on a board of Qh-7s-Tc-2c-6s. The Small Blind (Flashlight), who was the pre-flop aggressor, has checked on all three streets (flop, turn, and river). Pro's bet of 750 into a 2800 pot is small, offering favorable pot odds of roughly 4:1. With pocket nines on a relatively dry Jack-high board, my hand functions as a bluff-catcher. Checking might induce bluffs from missed draws, but it also allows worse made hands like QQ and KK to check back for a free showdown. By betting his remaining stack, I force him to make a tough decision with his high pocket pairs, extracting maximum value.

I am in the Big Blind with 8s 5d, which is a very weak offsuit hand. Flash has raised to 3x the big blind from the Button. Defending with 85o against a 3x raise, especially out of position, is a losing play in the long run. Folding is the standard and correct decision.
KQo is a premium starting hand in heads-up play. As the button (SB), raising to 2.5x the big blind takes control of the hand, builds the pot, and puts pressure on the big blind.

I am in the Big Blind with Js6s, which is a decent suited connector/one-gapper in a heads-up situation. The opponent, Pro, has raised to 250 from the Button/Small Blind. This is a standard open. To call, I only need to commit an additional 150 chips into a pot that will be 500, giving me 3.33:1 pot odds. Given the playability of suited J-high hands in position post-flop (or even out of position in this heads-up structure) and the fact that we have nearly identical stacks, folding is too tight. I will call to see a flop.

In this heads-up situation, I am out of position as the Big Blind against the pre-flop aggressor, Pro. The flop (10h 2s 5s) gives me a backdoor spade flush draw and one overcard (the Jack). Standard play here is to check to the raiser to see how they react. I want to realize my equity cheaply or potentially see a free turn card if they decide to check back.

I missed the flop completely and do not have a backdoor flush draw. With just two overcards on a board that contains a flush draw, checking back is the most prudent play. It allows me to realize my equity for free, keeps the pot small, and avoids getting check-raised, which would force me to fold my equity.

I have picked up a spade flush draw on the turn. Since the flop was checked through, neither player has shown much strength yet. The King is a card that often favors the pre-flop aggressor's range. I will check to maintain pot control and see how Pro reacts. I'm happy to take a free card to try and hit my flush or possibly call a standard-sized bet.

I turned top pair with a strong kicker after the flop checked through. The opponent checked again, indicating they likely don't have a very strong hand, though they might have picked up some equity like a weaker King, a Ten, or a flush/straight draw. Betting here is crucial for value and to protect my hand. A bet size around 70% of the pot (350) will charge draws appropriately and extract value from worse made hands.

On the turn, I have a flush draw (Js 6s on Th 2s 5s Kc). After the flop checked through, Pro's bet of 350 into a 500 pot is a bit over half-pot. I am getting roughly 2.4:1 on a call. With nine outs to the flush, plus possibly hitting a Jack for top pair (though a Jack could easily be behind a King or a Ten), I have enough equity to continue. I will call the bet to try and hit my flush on the river, or potentially win at showdown if I hit a Jack and Pro is bluffing with air.

On the river, the board is Th 2s 5s Kc Jd. I have pair of Jacks with a 6 kicker. The Jack on the river gave me second pair (below the King). Since the flop was checked through and Pro bet the turn when the King appeared, Pro likely has either a King, a draw, or is bluffing. By checking, I allow Pro to check back with worse hands that have showdown value or bluff with missed draws. Betting doesn't accomplish much as I am unlikely to be called by worse hands and will almost certainly be called by better ones (like a King or a straight). I will check and look to see a cheap showdown.

With top pair and a Queen kicker on a board that contains several straight and two-pair possibilities (T-J-K), checking behind on the river is the safest play. The opponent's turn call indicates they connected with the board in some way, potentially holding a hand like JT, KT, or a straight draw that just got there. By checking, I control the size of the pot, avoid a potential check-raise from a stronger hand, and comfortably realize my strong showdown value against missed flush draws or weaker one-pair hands.

I have been dealt Pocket Aces (Ad Ah), the strongest starting hand in Texas Hold'em. With only one opponent left (Pro), my goal is to build a large pot while not scaring them off immediately, though a raise is mandatory here. I am in the Small Blind/Button position. Pro has a slightly larger stack, so there is significant potential for a massive double-up if the board runout is favorable. I will raise to 300, which has been my standard opening size throughout the tournament, to keep my range balanced and induce a call or a re-raise from Pro.

I am in the Big Blind with AKo, a premium hand, against a button open. A 3-bet is optimal to build the pot, extract value, and take the initiative out of position. A sizing of 1000 is about 3.3x the open, which is standard for an OOP 3-bet.

I hold Pocket Aces (Ad Ah), the strongest possible starting hand. Pro has 3-bet my opening raise to 1000. In a heads-up situation with effective stacks of 140 big blinds, I want to build a large pot immediately to extract maximum value from strong hands in Pro's range like KK, QQ, or AK, and to protect my equity. I will 4-bet to 2600, which is a standard size that prices Pro in to continue with their stronger range while keeping the pot-to-stack ratio favorable for a potential commitment on later streets.

Facing a 4-bet out of position, AKo is too strong to fold, but 5-bet shoving risks isolating ourselves against a range that dominates us (AA, KK) while folding out all of the opponent's 4-bet bluffs. Calling the 4-bet is optimal as it keeps the opponent's weaker hands in the pot, allows us to realize our equity, and keeps the SPR manageable for post-flop play.

I called a 4-bet out of position pre-flop, so my opponent has the range and nut advantage on this low, somewhat disconnected board. With Ace-King high and no heart draw, I missed the flop completely. The standard and optimal play in a 4-bet pot out of position is to check to the pre-flop aggressor. Leading out here would be spewy and unbalanced. I will check and evaluate my opponent's action.

I have the best possible overpair (AA) on a relatively safe board of 2h-Tc-4h. I also hold the Ace of hearts, giving me the nut flush draw as a backup. The pot is already 5,200 and my remaining stack is 9,150, making the Stack-to-Pot Ratio (SPR) approximately 1.76. In this low SPR situation, I want to commit my stack against Pro's range, which likely consists of hands like JJ, QQ, KK, or possibly AK/AQs. By betting 2,100 (about 40% of the pot), I extract value from Pro's marginal made hands and draws while setting up a potential all-in on the turn.

In a 4-bet pot out of position, missing the flop completely without any backdoor flush draw makes my hand a clear fold against a continuation bet. The opponent's range heavily consists of overpairs (JJ+) and sets (TT), which crush my Ace-high. Calling would be unprofitable as I have poor equity and poor playability on future streets.

JTo is a solid starting hand in heads-up play from the button. Raising allows me to take the initiative and either win the pot pre-flop or have the betting lead going into the flop. I will raise to 2.5x the big blind.

In a heads-up situation, while ranges are much wider, Queen-Four offsuit is still quite weak to defend against a 2.5x open from the button. I currently have a chip lead and there is no need to commit chips with a hand that has poor playability and is likely dominated by the opponent's opening range. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I am on the button (Small Blind) with Qs 6d. In a heads-up situation against 'Pro', Q6o is a hand well within the standard opening range. Raising to 300 has been my consistent open-raise sizing throughout the tournament, allowing me to take control of the pot early or win the blinds if the opponent folds. My stack lead provides a good cushion to maintain this aggression.

Pocket Tens is a premium starting hand, well ahead of a standard button opening range. 3-betting from the Big Blind is the best play to build the pot, extract value from worse hands, and take the initiative out of position. A 3-bet size around 3-3.5x the original raise is optimal.

I am facing a 3-bet from Pro in the Big Blind after my initial raise to 300. While I have the chip lead and will be in position for the rest of the hand if I call, Q6o is a relatively weak hand to defend against a significant re-raise of 10bb. Pro's 3-bet sizing to 1000 is consistent with their previous aggressive plays. Calling here would often lead to difficult spots on the flop where I am likely behind or have very little equity. Given that I want to protect my stack and wait for higher-equity opportunities, folding is the most disciplined play in this situation.

Pre-flop from the Button (Small Blind), I hold J6o. This is a very weak and unplayable offsuit hand, well outside the range of hands I want to be opening or calling with. Folding is the standard and correct play to preserve my stack.

I am on the button with T6o. While T6o is a relatively weak hand, I have a significantly larger stack than 'Pro' and the pot is already at 150 with only 50 more for me to call the big blind. However, in heads-up play, T6o is usually a fold or a raise rather than a call from the small blind to maintain initiative. Given the recent aggressive history (Hand #90 where I folded to a 3-bet), I will choose to fold this weak hand and wait for a better spot to apply pressure since I currently hold a stack advantage.
King-Queen offsuit is a premium starting hand in heads-up play. As the dealer/small blind, raising to 2.5x the big blind is standard to take the initiative and build the pot.

I am in the Big Blind with Qd6h. Pro, on the Button (Small Blind), has raised to 250. This is a standard 2.5x open. While Qd6h is not a particularly strong hand, I have a significant stack advantage (16450 vs 13200) and the price to call is only 150 more to win a pot of 350. However, out of position, Qd6h plays poorly against a standard raising range. Looking at previous hands, Pro has been fairly active. Defending with a weak Queen-high is marginal, but given the pot odds (2.3:1) and the fact that we are head-to-head, I could occasionally call. However, consistently folding these weak holdings out of position is a safer way to maintain my stack lead and wait for better spots to exploit Pro's tendencies. I will fold this time.

I have AhQh on the button, which is a premium hand in heads-up play. My standard opening raise size has been 300 throughout the tournament, and I see no reason to deviate here. I want to build a pot with a strong hand and maintain my aggressive image. Pro has been folding to many of these raises lately, but I am happy to take the blinds or play a pot in position with this hand.

Holding a low pocket pair (33) in the Big Blind facing a 3x open from the Button, calling to set-mine is the standard and most profitable play. Both my opponent and I have deep stacks (over 130bb), giving me excellent implied odds if I hit a set on the flop. Out of position, 33 is easy to play post-flop: I can confidently check-fold if I miss and continue aggressively if I hit my set.

I flopped bottom set on a wet board with a flush draw present. As the pre-flop caller playing out of position, the standard and most profitable line is to check to the pre-flop raiser. This allows Flash to continuation bet with his bluffs, top pairs, or draws, at which point I can spring a check-raise to build the pot, extract maximum value, and protect my hand against the numerous draws on this board.

On this Jack-high flop (Jh 3c 6c), I have two overcards and a backdoor heart draw. Pro has checked to me, and as the pre-flop aggressor, a continuation bet is the standard play to represent a strong hand like a Jack or a high pocket pair. My hand still has significant equity against their range, and I can often take the pot down right now if they missed the flop entirely. I will bet 350 into the 600 pot, which is a standard size that puts pressure on their marginal holdings without over-committing.

I flopped bottom set on a wet board (Jh 3c 6c) with a flush draw and straight draws available. Flash made a continuation bet of 350. This is the perfect spot for a check-raise to build the pot for my premium hand and to charge his draws, top pairs, and overpairs to see the turn. Raising to around 3.5x his bet gets maximum value and defines the hand early while protecting my equity.

I have two overcards (Ace and Queen) and the backdoor nut flush draw with the Ace of hearts. Pro's check-raise on this board (Jh 3c 6c) is a strong move that typically represents a Jack, a set, or a draw. Given that we are heads-up, my hand still has significant equity against a wide range of hands he could be playing this way. I am getting approximately 2.6-to-1 on a call (800 to call into a 2100 pot), and I have the advantage of position for the remainder of the hand. Folding would be too tight given the strength of my overcards and backdoor potential, and raising further would likely only be called by hands that have me crushed. Calling allows me to see how the turn develops.

The turn pairs the Jack, improving my flopped bottom set to a full house (Threes full of Jacks). This is an incredibly strong hand that crushes any Jx hand my opponent might hold, as those hands only make three of a kind. Since Flash called my check-raise on the flop, his range heavily features flush draws, overpairs (QQ-AA), and Jx hands. I want to continue building the pot to extract maximum value, particularly from trip Jacks which will never fold. Betting around half the pot keeps his calling range wide, including stubborn draws and overpairs, while perfectly setting up a river shove.

On the turn, the Jack of Spades pairs the board. Pro's line of check-raising the flop and then leading out for a significant bet (1,500 into 2,900) on the turn is indicative of extreme strength, likely representing a Jack, a full house (33, 66), or a very strong draw that is now semi-bluffing. While my Ace-Queen had some merit on the flop, the pairing of the Jack makes it less likely that my overcards are currently the best hand, and even if I hit an Ace or Queen on the river, I could still be trailing a Jack. The price to call is 1,500 to win a total pot that would be 5,900, requiring roughly 25% equity. Given the strength shown by Pro, continuing with only Ace-high is too risky and lacks sufficient showdown value. I will fold and preserve my chip lead.

Q4o is a weak offsuit hand, even for a button open in heads-up play. While we would have position post-flop, the hand lacks connectivity and high-card strength. Folding is the most standard and solid choice here to avoid putting chips in with marginal equity.

Pocket Sixes is a strong pocket pair in a heads-up situation, well ahead of an average random hand. I will follow my standard opening procedure of raising to 300 from the button to build the pot and maintain an aggressive image.

I am dealt 54 offsuit in the big blind facing a 3x open raise from the button. This hand is too weak to defend out of position. It lacks high card strength, suitedness, and significant playability to justify calling or 3-betting against a competent opponent's opening range.
Q3o is a very weak offsuit hand. From the Small Blind/Button, it is an easy fold as it lacks high card strength, suitedness, and connectivity. I will save my chips for a more playable starting hand.

In heads-up play, the button has a significant advantage by acting last on all subsequent streets. While 8-6 offsuit is a marginal hand, I have been consistently opening for 300 from the small blind to apply pressure and take advantage of my positional lead. I will continue this strategy here; if the opponent 3-bets, I can easily fold, but if they call, I can use my position to navigate the post-flop play.

Facing a 3bb raise out of position, A5o is a marginal hand that struggles with playability post-flop. While it holds an Ace blocker and could be used as a 3-bet bluff occasionally, folding is the standard, lower-variance play against a 3x open. I'll let this one go and look for better spots to commit chips.

A4o is a strong enough hand to open-raise from the button in heads-up play. Having an Ace blocks premium hands and we have good fold equity pre-flop. I'll make a standard raise to 250 to take the initiative and potentially steal the blinds, or play a post-flop pot with position and a decent high card.

I have been dealt Pocket Queens, which is a premium hand, especially in a heads-up situation. My opponent (Pro) has raised from the button to 250. To maximize value and protect my hand, I will put in a 3-bet. A standard 3-bet size here is approximately 3 to 4 times the original raise. I will raise to 900 to build the pot and maintain initiative.

My hand is A4o, which is a marginal hand to defend against a 3-bet. While I have position post-flop, A4o plays poorly in 3-bet pots due to reverse implied odds when hitting an Ace, as the opponent's 3-betting range often contains stronger Aces. Folding is the most prudent and EV-preserving action here.

In a heads-up situation from the button (Small Blind), Queen-Ten offsuit (QTo) is a very strong hand. I have a stack of 15,500 compared to Pro's 14,350. Standard play is to raise to 3x the big blind to take control of the pot and leverage position. Raising to 300 is a consistent sizing I have used throughout the tournament.
Q4o is a very weak starting hand, especially facing a 3x raise out of position. Folding is the most straightforward and mathematically sound decision to preserve my stack.

Ad2d is a suited Ace, which is well above average in a heads-up scenario and definitely worth raising from the button. My standard opening size from the small blind/button is 2.5x the big blind, or 250 chips.

I am holding QsJs in the Big Blind. This is a premium suited broadway hand in heads-up play, well ahead of a standard button opening range. While calling is an option to keep the pot small, I prefer to 3-bet here for value and to take control of the hand. Given the recent history of aggressive pre-flop exchanges between myself and Pro, a raise to 900 puts pressure on his opening range and allows me to define the hand immediately.

My opponent has 3-bet to 900 after my standard open. While A2s is suited and has an Ace, it plays poorly against a 3-bet calling range due to reverse implied odds when hitting an Ace, as I'll often be out-kicked. Calling leaves me in a tough spot on many boards. Given the 3.6x sizing of the 3-bet, I will comfortably fold and wait for a better spot to continue or 4-bet.

I am on the button with Ten-Seven offsuit. My opponent has been folding to a lot of my pre-flop raises recently (Hands 96, 98, 100). Raising here is a standard play to maintain aggression and take advantage of the current momentum. I will stick with my standard opening size of 300.
J8o is a weak offsuit hand, and we are out of position facing a standard 3x open from the button. The correct play is to simply fold this hand and wait for a better spot to invest chips.

J9o is a marginal offsuit hand. While it can be opened on the button in heads-up play, Flash has been very aggressive with pre-flop 3-bets recently (as seen in hands 99 and 101). Folding here is a safe, zero-risk option that avoids getting into a tricky spot with a mediocre hand against an aggressive opponent.

I am on the button with Ace-Queen suited, which is a premium starting hand in heads-up play. My standard opening raise throughout this tournament has been 3x the big blind (300 chips). I want to build the pot with my value hand and maintain aggression against 'Pro', who has been folding to many of my pre-flop raises recently.

JTo is a perfectly reasonable hand to defend heads-up against a frequent button raiser. Facing a 3x open and getting 2:1 odds, calling to see a flop is a standard play that allows us to realize our equity against a wide range without inflating the pot unnecessarily out of position.
I missed the flop completely with Jack-high on a low, uncoordinated board. The standard line out of position as the pre-flop caller is to check to the raiser and evaluate their action.

I have Ace-Queen high on a very dry board of 7-4-2. As the pre-flop aggressor, I should follow up with a continuation bet after Pro checks. This board is unlikely to have improved Pro's range significantly, and a bet of roughly half the pot puts pressure on their non-pairing hands like K-high or J-high. Additionally, I hold two overcards and a backdoor diamond flush draw, giving me equity if the bet is called.

I completely missed the flop, having only Jack-high with no strong draws on a dry 7-4-2 rainbow board. Facing a continuation bet from the pre-flop raiser while out of position, I have essentially no equity to continue. Folding is the only sensible choice here.

Pocket deuces are the lowest pair, but heads-up any pocket pair is a strong starting hand. I will open with a standard 2.5x raise (250) from the button to take the initiative, potentially win the pot uncontested, and build the pot in case I hit a set on the flop.

I am in the Big Blind with 3c 2h, which is one of the weakest starting hands in poker. The Small Blind (Pro) has raised to 250. While the pot odds are 30% to call, this hand realizes its equity extremely poorly, especially out of position. I have been aggressive recently with 3-bets, and there's no reason to defend or bluff with the absolute bottom of my range when I have a comfortable chip lead. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I am on the button with Q5o. Looking at recent hands, Pro has been folding to most pre-flop raises (Hands #100, #102, #104). Maintaining my aggressive image from the button is key here to pick up the blinds. A standard 3x raise to 300 should be effective given Pro's recent tight-folding behavior.

Queen-Seven suited is a marginal hand that plays poorly out of position against an aggressive pre-flop raiser. While Flash opens frequently on the button, Q7s can suffer from reverse implied odds when hitting a Queen, and realizing its equity out of position is difficult. Folding is the disciplined play to avoid a potentially sticky post-flop situation.

With a small pocket pair (5d 5h) on the Button, opening with a standard raise to 2.5x BB (250) is a solid play. It gives us a chance to win the blinds immediately or play a pot in position where we can flop a highly deceptive and strong set.

I am in the Big Blind with 6-3 suited. The opponent on the Button (Pro) has raised to 2.5x the big blind (250). While 6-3 suited has some playability and the pot odds (call 150 to win a total pot of 500) suggest I need about 30% equity, playing such a weak hand out of position against an aggressive opponent is generally a losing proposition in the long run. Given my current chip lead and the fact that there are 43 hands remaining, I prefer to wait for better spots rather than defending with the bottom of my range.

I am on the button with JTs, a very strong hand in heads-up play. Standard play is to raise to 3x the big blind to build the pot and take control of the hand. My opponent has been folding to many of my pre-flop raises recently, and JTs plays well even if called.

Facing a 3x open from the opponent, Q4 suited is too weak to profitably defend from the big blind, especially being out of position. Folding is the standard and safest play here to preserve the stack for better opportunities.

I have a mid-to-low pocket pair (66) on the button. This is a strong starting hand in heads-up play, well ahead of the average random hand. A standard open raise to 2.5x the big blind (250) applies pressure, takes control of the pot, and allows me to either win the blinds outright or evaluate my options post-flop.

I am in the Big Blind with Jack-Ten offsuit, which is a very solid hand for heads-up play against a standard button raise of 2.5x. My opponent 'Pro' has a stack of 13,400, while I have 16,250. Given the stack depths and the strength of JTo, folding is out of the question. While I could 3-bet to put pressure on him, JTo plays well post-flop, and I don't necessarily want to bloat the pot out of position against a player who has shown aggression in previous hands. Calling allows me to see a flop and utilize the strength of my hand in a controlled pot.

I have flopped top two pair (Jacks and Tens) on an extremely coordinated board featuring both straight and flush draws. While my hand is very strong, it is also vulnerable to many turn cards. I will check to the pre-flop aggressor, 'Pro', to see if he continues his aggression. My plan is to check-raise any standard continuation bet to extract value from his draws and overpairs while protecting my hand from the many scary cards that could peel on the turn.

The 9h Jh Ts board connects heavily with my opponent's pre-flop calling range, giving them many combinations of straights, two pairs, top pairs, and strong draws. My pocket sixes are effectively an underpair with very little chance to improve, and a continuation bet is extremely likely to face resistance or be called. Checking behind controls the pot size and avoids throwing away chips in a highly unfavorable spot.

The turn Queen is a very dangerous card as it completes several possible straights (any King or any Eight). Although I have top two pair (Jacks and Tens), the board is now extremely coordinated. My opponent checked back the flop, which could indicate a range of hands from weak made hands to slow-played monsters or draws. Given the board texture, I will check to see how the 'Pro' responds. If he bets, I have to be extremely cautious as my two pair is now effectively a bluff catcher or a draw to a full house.

The turn puts a four-card straight on the board (9-T-J-Q). Any King or 8 makes a straight, and any pair or Ace-high beats my pocket sixes. With virtually zero showdown value and a high likelihood that my opponent's range connects with these broadway cards, attempting a bluff is extremely risky. Checking behind again controls the pot and safely navigates to the river without risking unnecessary chips.

On this board (9-T-J-Q-7), any King or any Eight makes a straight. Although I have two pair (Jacks and Tens), the board is extremely coordinated and my hand is essentially a bluff-catcher now. Pro raised pre-flop but checked both the flop and the turn. This could indicate he has a hand with some showdown value that didn't want to bet into a dangerous board, such as a Queen or a Jack, or he could be trapping with a straight. By checking the river, I am looking to get to a cheap showdown. If Pro bets, I will have to evaluate the sizing, but checking is the most prudent play to control the pot size on such a connected board.

The board has a 4-card straight (9-T-J-Q), meaning any King or Eight makes a straight. My pocket sixes are an underpair to the board's main cards. Since my opponent has checked all three streets, they likely have a very weak hand, such as Ace-high, a busted flush draw, or a low pocket pair. I already beat Ace-high and busted draws. Betting here is risky because it will likely only get called by hands that beat me (like a higher pair, a straight, or someone trapping), while worse hands will simply fold. Checking behind is the safest and most logical play to see a cheap showdown.

J9s is a strong starting hand in heads-up play, well within my opening range from the button. I have been consistently raising to 300 total throughout this match, which has frequently induced folds from Pro or allowed me to see flops with an initiative. I will maintain this sizing to keep my range balanced and apply pressure to the big blind.

With pocket threes in the big blind facing a 3x open from the button, calling to set-mine is a standard and mathematically sound play. The effective stack size is deep enough (133 BBs) to justify the implied odds if we hit our set, and we have reasonable equity against a wide button opening range.

I flopped bottom set on a wet, dynamic board (K-J-3 with two diamonds). Being out of position against the pre-flop raiser, the standard and most profitable play is to check my entire range to the aggressor. This allows him to continuation bet with his strong hands, draws, and bluffs. Once he bets, I plan to check-raise to build the pot and charge draws, as there are many turn cards that could kill my action or complete his draws.

On a board of Kd-Jh-3d, I have flopped middle pair with a 9 kicker. Pro checked to me, which is a standard move for the big blind after calling a pre-flop raise. While the board contains a King, my hand is strong enough to bet for value and to protect against various draws (like diamonds or straight draws). I have consistently used a sizing of 350 in similar spots throughout this match to maintain pressure and gather information. If called, I can reassess on the turn; if raised, I will likely fold given my weak kicker and the presence of the King.

We flopped bottom set on a very wet and coordinated board (K-J-3 with two diamonds). The opponent fired a continuation bet of 350. Since there are numerous straight draws and flush draws in his range, and he can easily commit chips with hands like AK, KQ, KJ, or strong combo draws, we must check-raise to extract maximum value and protect our hand from scare cards on the turn.

On this King-high coordinated board (Kd-Jh-3d), my middle pair of Jacks with a nine kicker is extremely vulnerable. After I bet 350, Pro check-raised to 1200, which is a significant increase. This action strongly suggests Pro has at least a King, two pair, or a set, or is semi-bluffing with a strong diamond draw. Given that my kicker is weak and the board offers several ways for me to be dominated, calling the raise would likely lead to difficult decisions on the turn and river with a marginal hand. With a comfortable stack lead, I choose to fold and wait for a better opportunity rather than risking more chips in a spot where I am likely behind or facing high equity draws.

K4s is a respectable starting hand from the Button in heads-up play. Opening with a standard raise to 2.5x (250 chips) asserts aggression, allows me to steal the blinds frequently, and builds a pot in position with a hand that can flop well with flush and top pair potential.

I am in the Big Blind with 3c 6h, which is an extremely weak starting hand. Pro has raised to 2.5bb (250 chips) from the Small Blind/Button. While the pot odds are roughly 2.6:1, 36o performs very poorly post-flop and out of position. Given that I currently hold a chip lead and we are at hand 111 of 150, I should minimize risk and avoid playing low-equity hands out of position. Folding is the standard and safest play here.

I have a pocket pair (3h, 3c) on the button. Small pocket pairs are valuable in heads-up play, and my standard opening raise from this position has been 300 chips. This allows me to take the initiative and potentially win the blinds immediately or see a flop with the betting lead.
I am dealt an unplayable offsuit hand (9d 5c) in the big blind facing a raise from the button. This is a very easy fold as 95o has terrible equity and poor playability post-flop.

7d5d is a suited one-gapper, which plays well heads-up from the button. It has good post-flop potential for straights, flushes, and bluffs. I will open with my standard raise size of 2.5x to take the initiative and potentially win the blinds right now, or play a pot in position with a disguised hand.

I hold Ah Th in the Big Blind, which is a premium starting hand in a heads-up match. The opponent raised to 250 from the Small Blind. Given the strength of my hand and the need to play a pot in position or at least with the initiative, I will 3-bet (re-raise) to 850 for value.

Facing a 3-bet from the Big Blind, 7d5d is a bit too weak to call profitably. While it has some playability in position, the gap and low rank of the cards leave it vulnerable to domination and frequently missing the flop. Folding here is the standard, conservative play to preserve chips.

I am on the button with T8o. This is a standard opening range hand in heads-up play, even if technically it's a weak-ish hand. My stack size is 161.5 big blinds, and Pro has 137 big blinds.
